---
title: "Populations, variables, units, uncertainty"
output:
  beamer_presentation:
    theme: "default"
    colortheme: "default"
    fonttheme: "default"
  ioslides_presentation: default
  slidy_presentation: default
header-includes:
- \usepackage{hyperref}
- \usepackage{caption}
- \usepackage[normalem]{ulem}
- \definecolor{links}{HTML}{2A1B81}
- \hypersetup{colorlinks,linkcolor=,urlcolor=links}
colorlinks: true
linkcolor: blue
urlcolor: blue
---

```{r, echo = FALSE}
library(knitr);
opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE);
```



## Populations and samples


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "30+ bird silhouettes in a dotted box called 'population'. Within this, four birds are in a grey box called 'sample'.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/population_sample_1.png")
```


## Types of variables: definitions

\LARGE
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Categorical:} Fixed number of options
\begin{itemize} \LARGE
\item Nominal: No inherent order
\item Ordinal: Inherent order
\end{itemize} \vspace{6mm}
\item \textbf{Quantitative:} Numbers meaningful
\begin{itemize} \LARGE
\item Discrete: Limited number of values
\item Continuous: Any real number
\end{itemize}
\end{itemize}
\vspace{3mm}
All of these measurements have uncertainty



## Metrology: The science of measurement

\LARGE

Metrology focuses on measurement accuracy, precision, and units$^{1}$

- **Measurement**: Determination of the properties of a unit of observation \pause
- **Measurand**: The unknown *true* value of what we want to measure \pause
- **Measurement error**: Difference between the measurement and the measurand


\footnotetext[1]{Rabinovich, SG. 2013. Evaluating Measurement Accuracy: A Practical Approach. Springer Science \& Business Media. \href{https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-60125-0}{10.1007/978-3-319-60125-0}}


## Metrology: The science of measurement

\LARGE

Metrology originally for economic activities$^{1}$

\begin{itemize}
\item Need standardised measurements for trade
\item Standardised to a measurement \textbf{unit:}
\begin{itemize} \LARGE
\item Unit of length (e.g., cm)
\item Unit of mass (e.g., mg)
\item Unit of time (e.g., seconds)
\end{itemize}
\item The `unit' defines what is 1
\end{itemize}
\vspace{3mm}

\footnotetext[1]{Fanton, JP. 2019. International Journal of Metrology and Quality Engineering. \href{https://www.metrology-journal.org/articles/ijmqe/pdf/2019/01/ijmqe180023.pdf}{10:5}.}


## Metrology: The science of measurement

\LARGE

Measuring wing length in centimetres (cm)

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Drawing of a bird wing being measured by a ruler.", fig.cap = "", out.width="90%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/measurement.jpg")
```

- Measure 7.28 *units* of length (cm)
- Measurand is the *true* wing length
- Repeated measures estimate *error*

\footnotetext[1]{\textbf{Image:} Reichenow, A. 1913. (\href{https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:BirdMorphometrics_(wing_measurement).jpg}{Public domain}).}


## Accuracy and precision

\Huge

- **Accuracy** is how close a measurement is to the *true* value we want to measure.$^{1}$ \pause
- **Precision** is how consistent a measurement will be if replicated multiple times.$^{2}$

\footnotetext[1]{Rabinovich, SG. 2013. Evaluating Measurement Accuracy: A Practical Approach. Springer Science \& Business Media. \href{https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-60125-0}{10.1007/978-3-319-60125-0}}
\footnotetext[2]{Wardlaw, AC. 1985. Practical Statistics for Experimental Biologists (p. 290). John Wiley \& Sons, Chichester, UK.}


## Accuracy and precision

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Four targets, A, B, C, and D, are shown side-by-side, wach with five points scatter on top of them. Points in A are close together and near the centre, points in B are far apart but around the center, points in C are close together but away from the centre, and points in D are far apart and away from the centre.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/accuracy_vs_precision.png")
```

\LARGE

Measurements can be as follows:

- **A:** Accurate and precise
- **B:** Accurate but not precise
- **C:** Not accurate but precise
- **D:** Not accurate nor precise


\footnotetext[1]{\textbf{Image:} Willighagen, E. 2014. (\href{https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Accuracy-vs-precision-nl.svg}{Public domain}).}

## Units of measurement

\Large

Units of *measurement* different from units of *observation*

- Measurement unit is a defined measurement quantity
- Standardised units of measurement ensure accuracy$^{1}$
- Ideally based on fundamental constants of nature$^{2}$
- Measurement units can be base or derived
- Seven standardised base units


\footnotetext[1]{Quinn, TJ. 1995. Metrologia, 31:515–527. \href{https://doi.org/10.1088/0026-1394/31/6/011}{10.1088/0026-1394/31/6/011}}
\footnotetext[2]{Stock, M, et al. 2019. Metrologia, 56:\href{https://iopscience.iop.org/article/10.1088/1681-7575/ab28a8}{022001}} 


## Kilogram was *defined* by a mass of metal

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "A steel cylinder is shown enclosed in glass container that is enclosed by another glass container", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/kg.jpg")
```


\footnotetext[1]{\textbf{Image:} National Inst. of Standards \& Technology. 2021. (\href{https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:U.s.-kilogram-masses.jpg}{Public domain}).}


## Kilogram was *defined* by a mass of metal

\Large

Standardising mass to a physical object problematic

- If object changes, so does the unit of mass
- Base unit affects all other measurements 

\pause

Kilogram redefined in 2019$^{1}$:

- Atomic transition frequency ($\Delta v_{Cs}$)
- Speed of light ($c$)
- Planck constant ($h$)

$$1\:kg = \left(1.4755213 \times 10^{40}\right)\frac{h \Delta v_{Cs}}{c^{2}}$$


\footnotetext[1]{Stock, M et al. 2019. Metrologia \href{https://iopscience.iop.org/article/10.1088/1681-7575/ab0013}{56:022001.}}


## Base units of SI measurements

\Large

| Measured Quantity           | Name of SI Unit     | Symbol       |
|-----------------------------|---------------------|--------------|
| Mass                        | kilogram            | kg           |
| Length                      | metre               | m            |
| Time                        | second              | s            |
| Electric current            | ampere              | A            |
| Temperature                 | kelvin              | K            |
| Amount of a substance       | mole                | mol          |
| Luminous intensity          | candela             | cd           |


## Derived SI measurements

| Measured Quantity     | Name of Unit            | Symbol     | Definition in SI Units     |
|-----------------------|-------------------------|------------|----------------------------|
| Area                  | square metre            | $A$        | m^2^                       |
| Volume                | cubic metre             | $V$        | m^3^                       |
| Speed                 | metre per second        | $v$        | m s^-1^                    |
| Force                 | newton                  | $N$        | m kg s^-2^                 |
| Pressure              | pascal                  | $Pa$       | m^-1^ kg s^-2^             |
| Energy                | joule                   | $J$        | m^2^ kg s^-2^              |

\Large

Derived units of measurement built from base units


## Units versus labels

\LARGE


\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Unit} defines a magnitude of a quantity
\begin{itemize} \LARGE 
\vspace{-0.3cm}\item 1 kilogram
\vspace{-0.3cm}\item 1 metre
\end{itemize} \vspace{3mm}
\item \textbf{Label} describes the data type
\begin{itemize} \LARGE 
\vspace{-0.3cm}\item soil mass
\vspace{-0.3cm}\item flight distance
\end{itemize} \vspace{3mm}
\item \textbf{Counts} do not have units
\begin{itemize} \LARGE 
\vspace{-0.3cm}\item 20 glaciers
\vspace{-0.3cm}\item 800 seeds
\end{itemize}
\end{itemize}


## Measurement uncertainty propagation

\LARGE

Nothing measured with perfect accuracy

- Noise in the measuring environment
- Mistakes made in measurement
- Limitations of measuring device

Measurement errors accumulate!

\vspace{2mm}

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Close up of a tape measure.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/tape_measure.jpg")
```

\footnotetext[1]{\textbf{Image:} Perkins, D. 2015.  (\href{https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Tape_measure_end.jpg}{Public domain}).}



## Measurement uncertainty propogation

\LARGE

Suppose we measured 2 stones separately

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Close up of an old fashioned scale.", fig.cap = "", out.width="90%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/scale.jpg")
```

Each measurement has a $\pm$ error

\footnotetext[1]{\textbf{Image:} Nijaki, N. 2011.  (\href{https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Balance_scale_IMGP9755.jpg}{Public domain}).}


## Measurement uncertainty propogation

\Huge

Combined measurement error

- Stone 1: $40 \pm 1.2 \:kg$
- Stone 2: $36 \pm 1.1 \:kg$

Combined mass: $40\:kg + 36\:kg = 76\:kg$

Combined measurement error?

## Measurement uncertainty propogation

\Large

Stone example:

$$Combined\:mass = Mass\:1 + Mass\:2$$

\pause

More generally:

$$Z = X + Y$$

\pause

With error ($E$):

$$(Z \pm E_Z) = (X  \pm E_X) + (Y \pm E_Y)$$

## Measurement uncertainty propogation

\Large

$$(Z \pm E_Z) = (X  \pm E_X) + (Y \pm E_Y)$$


If we solve for $E_{Z}$,

$$E_Z = \sqrt{E_X^2 + E_Y^2}.$$

For our two stones,

$$E_Z = \sqrt{1.2^2 + 1.1^2} = 1.63.$$


## Measurement uncertainty propogation {.t}

\LARGE

- Morning run: $4.5 \pm 0.3 \:km$
- Evening run: $3.8 \pm 0.2 \:km$

Combined error ($E_{Z}$) for total run length?

$$E_Z = \sqrt{E_X^2 + E_Y^2}.$$


## Measurement uncertainty propogation

\Large

$$Z = X \times Y$$

Combining errors different for multiplication

$$Z \pm E_Z = (X \pm E_X)(Y \pm E_Y).$$

\pause

If we isolate $E_{Z}$,

$$E_Z = Z \sqrt{\left( \frac{E_X}{X}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{E_Y}{Y}\right)^2}.$$

##  Measurement uncertainty propogation


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Sketch of a plot of land length 432 plus or minus 12.3 and width 162.4 plus or minus 5.6.", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/transect_1.png")
```



## Calculating uncertainty {.t}

\Large

$$E_Z = Z \sqrt{\left( \frac{E_X}{X}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{E_Y}{Y}\right)^2}.$$

\vspace{12mm}


- $X = 432$
- $Y = 162$
- $E_{X} = 12.3$
- $E_{Y} = 5.6$


## Calculating uncertainty {.t}

\Large

$$E_Z = Z \sqrt{\left( \frac{E_X}{X}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{E_Y}{Y}\right)^2}.$$



##  Measurement uncertainty propogation


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Sketch of a plot of land length 621.0 plus or minus 24.4 and width 566.1 plus or minus 22.1.", fig.cap = "", out.width="90%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/transect_2.png")
```


## Calculating uncertainty {.t}

\Large

$$E_Z = Z \sqrt{\left( \frac{E_X}{X}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{E_Y}{Y}\right)^2}.$$





## Working with jamovi


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Screenshot of the full interface of jamovi with some data loaded", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/jamovi_1.png")
```


## Working with jamovi


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Screenshot of the jamovi descriptives interface showing data summaries", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/jamovi_3.png")
```



## Working with jamovi


```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.alt = "Screenshot of the module options for jamovi", fig.cap = "", out.width="100%"}
knitr::include_graphics("img/jamovi_4.png")
```
